duration.$description: Primitive animation duration values. Three stops covering the full range from instant micro-interactions to deliberate transitions. Do not use these directly in UI — reference semantic motion tokens when available.

fast.$description: Instant feedback. Use for hover state color changes, focus ring appearance, and any interaction where the UI must feel immediately responsive. At 120ms, the transition is felt but not watched. Referenced by: motion.duration.instant.

normal.$description: Standard transition. Use for expand/collapse, dropdown menus, tooltip appearance, and most component state changes. Fast enough to feel snappy; slow enough to be legible. Referenced by: motion.duration.standard.

slow.$description: Deliberate transition. Use for page-level transitions, modal entry/exit, and large layout changes where the motion needs to be noticed and tracked by the eye. Do not use for simple hover or focus states. Referenced by: motion.duration.emphasized.

delay.$description: Primitive transition delay values. The default for every transition in this system is zero — ad-hoc delays produce sluggish, uncoordinated UI. Add new stops here only when a deliberate stagger or sequence requires it; never inline a delay value in UI code.

none.$description: No delay. The transition starts the moment the property changes. The default delay for every composite transition token in this system. Referenced by: motion.transition.* (all).

easing.$description: Cubic bezier easing curves. Match the easing to the direction of motion — elements entering should decelerate (ease-out), elements exiting should accelerate (ease-in). Default is general-purpose.

default.$description: General-purpose easing (CSS ease). A gentle acceleration and deceleration. Use when the motion has no clear directional intent. Referenced by: motion.easing.default.

in.$description: Ease-in. Starts slow, ends fast. Use for elements that are exiting or being dismissed. Referenced by: motion.easing.exit.

out.$description: Ease-out. Starts fast, decelerates to rest. Use for elements entering the screen. Referenced by: motion.easing.enter.

in-out.$description: Ease-in-out. Accelerates then decelerates symmetrically. Use for elements moving from one position to another within the viewport. Referenced by: motion.easing.move.
